title: Worksheet 7: Functions
description: Make your own Python tools using def, parameters, and return — then put them together.

problems.title: Python comes with tools — today you make new ones
problems.content:
You already use Python's built-in tools all the time:

print("hello")
len([1, 2, 3])
range(5)

Today you learn how to make your own tools. Once you've made one, you can use it as many times as you like — just like print.

The keyword that makes a new tool is def. It stands for define.
problems.footer: A function is a named piece of code you can run whenever you want.

problems.title: Your first function
problems.content:
This program defines a function called say_hi and then calls it. Look at the two parts:

  • def say_hi(): creates the tool
  • say_hi() uses the tool

The code under def only runs when you call the function with say_hi().
problems.task: Run the code and watch what happens.
problems.hint: Just press 'Run Code'. The function prints once because it's called once.

problems.title: Call it more than once
problems.content: Once a function is defined, you can call it as many times as you like. Each call runs the body again.
problems.task: Add two more say_hi() calls so the message prints three times in total.
problems.hint: Just write say_hi() on two new lines below.

rules.solutionCode:
def say_hi():
  print("Hi there!")

say_hi()
say_hi()
say_hi()

problems.title: Write your own function
problems.content: Your turn. Make a function called cheer that prints "Hooray!", then call it.
problems.starterCode:
# Define cheer here


# Call it here
problems.hint: Write def cheer():, then indent print("Hooray!") on the next line. Below the function, write cheer() to call it.

problems.title: Parameters — your tool takes an input
problems.content:
Just like print needs to know what to print, your tool can take an input too. The input goes in the brackets after the function name:

def greet(name):
  print("Hi, " + name)

greet("Ali")    # Hi, Ali
greet("Sam")    # Hi, Sam

The word inside the brackets — here, name — is called a parameter. When you call the function, the value you pass becomes that parameter inside the body.
problems.footer: Same function. Different inputs. Different output.

problems.title: Greet two friends
problems.content: Look at how greet uses the parameter name. Each call sends a different value in.
problems.task: Run the code and watch how the same function produces two different greetings.
problems.hint: Press Run. Notice the function prints twice — once for each call.

problems.title: Edit farewell
problems.content: Here's a function called farewell. The body says goodbye, but it's a bit boring.
problems.task: Change the body of farewell so it prints See you tomorrow, {name}! instead. Don't change the def line or the calls.
problems.hint: Change the f-string inside print(...) to f"See you tomorrow, {name}!".

rules.description: Output should contain 'See you tomorrow, Ali!'
Output should contain 'See you tomorrow, Sam!'

problems.title: Print vs return — give back an answer
problems.content:
Some Python tools do things. Others give back an answer:

  • print("hi") — shows hi on the screen. Doesn't give anything back.
  • len([1, 2, 3]) — gives back 3. You can save it: n = len([1, 2, 3]).

Your functions can do the same. Use return instead of print to give an answer back:

def shout(text):
  return text + "!"

x = shout("hi")    # x is now "hi!"
print(x)           # shows hi!

The next few problems use a spec table. It calls your function with different inputs and shows what it gave back. Your job is to make every row pass.
problems.footer: print SHOWS the answer. return GIVES IT BACK so you can use it later.

problems.title: Meet the spec table
problems.content: Here's a function loud that takes some text and gives back a louder version. Look at the spec table below the editor — it shows three calls and what each one should give back.
problems.task: Run the code. The spec table should fill in green ticks for every row.
problems.hint: Just press Run. The spec table calls loud("hi"), loud("yay"), and loud("oh") and checks each return value.

problems.title: Returning a number
problems.content: Functions can return numbers too, not just strings. Here's double — it gives back twice the number you pass in.
problems.task: Run the code and watch the spec table fill in.
problems.hint: Press Run. The spec table calls double(2), double(3), double(7) and checks each return value.

problems.title: Turn double into square
problems.content: You've seen double(n) return n * 2. Now make a function called square that returns n times itself: n * n.
problems.task: Rename the function to square and change the body so the spec table goes green.
problems.hint: Two changes: rename double to square, and change n * 2 to n * n.

problems.title: Two parameters
problems.content:
A function can take more than one input. Just list them inside the brackets, separated by a comma:

def combine(first, last):
  return first + " " + last

Now combine needs two values when you call it: combine("Sam", "Lee").
problems.task: Run the code and watch the spec table check two-parameter calls.
problems.starterCode:
def combine(first, last):
  return first + " " + last
problems.hint: Press Run. The spec table calls combine("Sam", "Lee"), combine("Pat", "Kim"), etc.

problems.title: Roll-call format
problems.content: School roll-calls usually go last name, first name — like "Lee, Sam".
problems.task: Change the body of combine so it returns the last name first, then a comma and a space, then the first name.
problems.starterCode:
def combine(first, last):
  return first + " " + last
problems.hint: Swap the order of first and last, and use ", " in the middle instead of " ".

problems.title: Don't forget to return
problems.content: This function computes the right answer but it never gives it back. The spec table shows None for every row — that's what Python uses when a function returns nothing.
problems.task: Add a return so the spec passes.
problems.hint: On the last line, write return result (with the same indent as result = ...).

problems.title: Write area from scratch
problems.content: Time to write your own. The area function takes a width and a height and gives back the area of the rectangle (width times height).
problems.task: Write a function called area with two parameters: w and h. Return w * h.
problems.hint: Start with def area(w, h):. On the next line (indented), write return w * h.

rules.description: Use return to give back the answer

problems.title: Tools using other tools
problems.content:
Once a function is defined, your other functions can use it too. That's how big programs get built — small tools combine into bigger ones.

def shout(text):
  return text + "!!!"

def double_shout(text):
  return shout(text) + " " + shout(text)

print(double_shout("yay"))   # yay!!! yay!!!

The next few problems give you a row of buttons next to your code. Each button calls your function with different inputs — you don't have to type the calls yourself. Just write the function, click Run, then click the buttons to try it out.
problems.footer: Combine small tools to build big ones.

problems.title: Watch stars run
problems.content: This function prints n stars on a line. The trick is that "*" * n repeats the star n times — you saw that in Worksheet 5.
problems.task: Click Run, then click each button below to see stars called with different values.
problems.hint: Press Run first to define the function. Then try the buttons — each one calls stars with a different number.

problems.title: Make a row
problems.content: stars only prints stars. Let's generalise it: a row function takes a character and a number, and prints that character repeated.
problems.task: Write row(char, n) so it prints char repeated n times. Then click the buttons to try it.
problems.hint: Two parameters: def row(char, n):. Body: print(char * n).

problems.title: Build a box
problems.content:
A box is just a row, repeated. The box function should print an n-by-n square of stars by calling row in a loop.

row is already defined for you — your box function should use it.
problems.task: Write box(n) that calls row("*", n) a total of n times. Click Run, then try the buttons.
problems.hint: Inside box, write a for loop: for i in range(n):, then indent row("*", n).

rules.description: Your box function should call row()

problems.title: Triangle
problems.content: A triangle starts with a row of 1 star, then 2 stars, then 3, all the way up to n. You can build it by calling row with different lengths inside a loop.
problems.task: Write triangle(n) that prints rows of stars, growing from 1 up to n.
problems.hint: Use for i in range(1, n+1): so i goes 1, 2, 3, ... up to n. Inside, call row("*", i).

rules.description: Your triangle function should call row()

problems.title: Design your own shape
problems.content:
Time to invent your own. Write a function that prints any shape you like — a tall pillar, a diamond, your initial, anything.

You can use row inside it, or just print directly. Whatever you make, give it a name and call it at the bottom so it shows in the output.
problems.task: Write your own function that prints a shape, then call it.
problems.hint: Try something like def pillar(n): with a loop that calls row("#", 1) n times. Or get creative!

rules.description: Call your function so it prints something

problems.title: Total of a list
problems.content: Let's combine functions with what you learned in Worksheet 6. Write a function called total that takes a list of prices and returns their sum.
problems.task: Write total(prices) that adds up all the prices in the list and returns the total.
problems.hint: Start a running total at 0. Use for price in prices: and add each price to the total. At the end, return the total.

rules.description: Use return to give back the total
